coredata.dc:title: Integrating the high peroxidase activity of carbon dots with easy recyclability: Immobilization on dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils and cholesterol detection
coredata.dc:creator: Bandi, Rajkumar
Alle, Madhusudhan
Dadigala, Ramakrishna
Park, Chan-Woo
Han, Song-Yi
Kwon, Gu-Joong
Kim, Jin-Chul
Lee, Seung-Hwan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are widely explored as peroxidase mimics in recent times. However, low catalytic activity, substrate induced aggregation in the reaction medium and difficulties in recovery and reuse are the major hitches. A novel strategy is proposed here to circumvent these constrains by immobilizing amine functionalized, Fe, N-doped CDs (FeNCDs) with high peroxidase activity on dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils (DACNF) via Schiff base reaction and reductive amination. FeNCDs with iron content of 5.37% and high peroxidase activity (specific activity of 3654 U/g) were prepared by simple hydrothermal reaction. FeNCDs exhibited a broad-spectrum peroxidase activity and steady-state kinetics revealed their better kinetic parameters (low Km and high Vmax) over horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and Ping-Pong mechanism similar to HRP. Two fold increment in the peroxidase activity is observed under UV irradiation and mechanistic studies revealed the hydroxyl radical generation. DACNF with 1.25 ± 0.12 mM/g aldehyde content and 90% yield were produced by the periodate oxidation and served as an excellent matrix for FeNCDs to retain the fluorescence and peroxidase activities. Colorimetric assays based on 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) oxidation offered linear ranges of 10 – 80 µM and 50 – 400 µM and detection limits of 0.70 and 3.72 μM for H2O2 and cholesterol respectively. The results of cholesterol detection in human serum samples were in well agreement with those of clinical laboratory. Further, the DACNF/FeNCDs nanohybrid presented a long shelf life and excellent reusability for 10 cycles. Fe content analysis by ICP-OES revealed the insignificant leaching of FeNCDs even after 10 cycles. As a whole, this stable and recyclable nanozyme with high catalytic activity presents a great promise for multiple applications including clinical diagnosis, environmental protection and antimicrobials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of FeNCDs, (b) corresponding particle-size distribution histogram; TEM image of (c) PCNF, and (d) DACNF; XRD patterns of (e) FeNCDs, (f) PCNF, DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs; FTIR spectra of (g) FeNCDs, (h) PCNF, DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs.

XPS analysis: (a) Survey scan, high resolution scans of (b) Fe 2p and (c) C1s of FeNCDs. (d) Comparative survey scans of DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs and (e) Comparative N1s peak of FeNCDs and DACNF/FeNCDs.

(a) Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of FeNCDs (inset: FeNCDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)); (b) Emission spectra of FeNCDs at various excitation wavelengths; Digital photographs of DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs films under (c) day light and (d) 365 nm UV light.

Peroxidase activity test of FeNCDs with (a) ABTS, (b) TMB, and (c) OPD as chromogenic substrates; inset: corresponding digital photographs showing the color changes; Steady state kinetic analysis: (d,g) Michaelis-Menten curves, and (e,h) Line weaver-Burk plots for H2O2 and ABTS respectively. Double reciprocal plots at different fixed concentrations of (f) ABTS and (i) H2O2.

(a) Kinetic plots of ABTS oxidation showing the linear portion at various amounts of FeNCDs (b) corresponding plot of bnanozyme vs FeNCDs mass; Probing hydroxyl radical formation: (c) Emission spectra of TA after 12 h incubation with 1 mM H2O2 and various amounts of FeNCDs (λex 315 nm) (d) RhB degradation by various groups in acetate buffer (pH 3.6) after incubation for 2 h (0.5 M H2O2, 30 µg FeNCDs, 20 µg RhB) (e) ESR spectra of H2O2+DMPO and FeNCDs+H2O2+DMPO systems in acetate buffer (pH 3.6) after incubation for 10 min (10 µg FeNCDs, 20 mM DMPO, 400 mM H2O2) (f) Effect of lighting conditions on the peroxidase activity of FeNCDs (FeNCDs 10 µg, H2O2 0.1 mM, ABTS 0.25 mM, ΔA = A417nm with catalyst – A417nm without catalyst, error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements).

(a) Absorption spectra of ABTS in the presence of various amounts of H2O2 (inset: corresponding photographic images) (b) corresponding plot of A417 nm vs H2O2 concentration showing linearity (c) Absorption spectra of ABTS in the presence of various amounts of Cholesterol (inset: corresponding photographic images) (d) corresponding plot of A417 nm vs Cholesterol concentration showing the linearity (e) selectivity of cholesterol detection (concentrations cholesterol 0.25 mM, all other 2.5 mM) (f) stability and (g) recyclability of DACNF/FeNCDs nanozyme.
Comparison of the kinetic parameters with natural enzyme and other nanozymes.
Comparison of the cholesterol detection performance of various nanozyme based colorimetric methods.
Total cholesterol determination in human serum samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are widely explored as peroxidase mimics in recent times. However, low catalytic activity, substrate induced aggregation in the reaction medium and difficulties in recovery and reuse are the major hitches. A novel strategy is proposed here to circumvent these constrains by immobilizing amine functionalized, Fe, N-doped CDs (FeNCDs) with high peroxidase activity on dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils (DACNF) via Schiff base reaction and reductive amination. FeNCDs with iron content of 5.37% and high peroxidase activity (specific activity of 3654 U/g) were prepared by simple hydrothermal reaction. FeNCDs exhibited a broad-spectrum peroxidase activity and steady-state kinetics revealed their better kinetic parameters (low Km and high Vmax) over horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and Ping-Pong mechanism similar to HRP. Two fold increment in the peroxidase activity is observed under UV irradiation and mechanistic studies revealed the hydroxyl radical generation. DACNF with 1.25 ± 0.12 mM/g aldehyde content and 90% yield were produced by the periodate oxidation and served as an excellent matrix for FeNCDs to retain the fluorescence and peroxidase activities. Colorimetric assays based on 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) oxidation offered linear ranges of 10 – 80 µM and 50 – 400 µM and detection limits of 0.70 and 3.72 μM for H2O2 and cholesterol respectively. The results of cholesterol detection in human serum samples were in well agreement with those of clinical laboratory. Further, the DACNF/FeNCDs nanohybrid presented a long shelf life and excellent reusability for 10 cycles. Fe content analysis by ICP-OES revealed the insignificant leaching of FeNCDs even after 10 cycles. As a whole, this stable and recyclable nanozyme with high catalytic activity presents a great promise for multiple applications including clinical diagnosis, environmental protection and antimicrobials.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes gained significant attention with their superior characteristics over natural enzymes and traditional enzyme mimics [1]. Among the wide variety of enzyme-mimicking nanozymes reported so far, peroxidase mimics gained special attention with their broad range of applications including biosensing, therapy and environmental remediation [2] [3]. Numerous nanomaterials including metals, metal oxides, metal-organic frame works and carbon nanomaterials were reported to possess peroxidase mimicking nature [4]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) as the newest member of carbon nanomaterials family gained tremendous attention as peroxidase mimics [5]. CDs are zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials with less than 10 nm of size, high aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and tuneable fluorescence [6–8]. Further they possess the advantages of simple synthesis, ease of functionalization and high stability. These enticing characteristics made CDs a good choice in broad application fields such as biomedicine, sensing, optoelectronics, catalysis, and forensic [9,10].

Since the pioneering work of Shi et al. [11], several researchers reported the peroxidase mimicking nature of CDs. However, the peroxidase activities were found low and strategies like doping with non-metal and metal atoms were proposed. So far, metal ion doping along with N is considered as the best approach to tune the intrinsic characters and improve the catalytic activity [12]. Among all metals, Iron doping is promising with the intrinsic Fenton's behavior, abundance and low cost. The common drawbacks of colloidal nanozymes remained with CDs also. For instance, a mechanistic study on peroxidase activity of CDs by Smita Das et al. revealed the aggregation of CDs in the presence of H2O2 and reduction in catalytic activity [13]. Though the precursors are readily available, tedious purification processes and low yields made CDs as expensive materials. The price of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of CDs family is ∼4 million USD/kg now [14]. Hence, recovery and reuse is also a matter of concern. However, with their ultra-small size and high aqueous solubility, it is extremely difficult to recover CDs by means of centrifugation or other techniques. Immobilization on a polymeric support is considered as one of the best approaches to overcome these limitations.

In view of the fascinating properties such as low density, high mechanical strength, surface functionalization, biodegradability, and renewability nanocellulose can best serve as a support for CDs immobilization [15–17]. Even though immobilization of CDs on various forms of nanocellulose is reported, fluorescence properties of CDs were the main focus [18]. For the first time our group reported the immobilization of peroxidase mimicking CDs on cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) by physical entrapment [19]. However, forming a chemical cross linkage is known to offer better attachment. Among the various chemical modifications of cellulose, periodate oxidation is considered as the mild reaction and it offers dialdehyde functions that can easily bind with amine functions of CDs via Schiff base reaction.

Cholesterol is an important lipid in humans, building block of hormonal system and involves in the synthesis of several vitamins [20]. However, the high and low levels of serum cholesterol are associated with a number of diseases [21]. Hence, developing an easy, sensitive and selective method for cholesterol quantification is necessary for point-of-care diagnosis. Compared to the tedious conventional techniques, nanozyme based colorimetric detections are known to offer several advantages. However, most of the previous studies reported the use of colloidal nanozyme. This might result in a various complications including nanoparticle aggregation in complex biological samples and development of a biological corona on particle surface leading to unpredictable results [22].

In the present work, to address these issues, a facile strategy of immobilizing peroxidase mimicking CDs on dialdehyde cellulose nanofibrils (DACNF) is proposed for the first time. Fe,N-doped CDs (FeNCDs) with surface amine functions were prepared and their peroxidase activity is explored first. Meanwhile, DACNF was prepared by periodate oxidation of CNF and FeNCDs were immobilized via Schiff base formation and reductive amination. Peroxidase activity of as prepared nanohybrid (DACNF/FeNCDs) was explored and applied to the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and cholesterol. Recyclability of the nanozyme is further explored.

Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine, FeCl2•4H2O, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylnediamine (OPD), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), 5,5-dimethyl N-oxide pyrroline (DMPO), 2-picoline borane, Cholesterol, Cholesterol oxidase, and Cholesterol esterase were purchased from sigma Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Pristine cellulose nanofibrils (PCNF) were commercially obtained from Cellulose Lab, Co., Ltd. (QC, Canada). All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

FeNCDs were prepared according to a previous report of NCDs with slight modifications [23]. Periodate oxidation of PCNF was performed according to a literature report with slight variation [24]. Complete details on characterization, determination of aldehyde content, Quantum yield calculation, and enzyme kinetics can be found in the supporting information (SI).

10 mL of 0.4% DACNF suspension was bath sonicated for 1 min and 800 µL of 1 mg/mL FeNCDs solution was added. After stirring for 1 hour, 2-picoline borane was added at a mole ratio of 2 (borane to aldehyde). After stirring for 6 h, the contents were washed at pump with 200 mL deionized water (DW), filtered and dried at pump. Advantech membrane filters with 47 mm diameter and 0.2 µ pore size, were used. Finally, the composite film was cut in to small strips of 6 mm diameter using an office punch.

100 µL of 10 mM ABTS and 100 µL of H2O2 of various concentrations were added to 1800 µL of acetate buffer (pH 3.6) taken in a 2 mL vial. A DACNF/FeNCDs strip was added to each vial and incubated at 30 °C for 20 min. The absorbance change of the solution was monitored using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Cholesterol detection was carried out as follows: firstly, 50 μL of 70 UN/mL cholesterol oxidase, 100 μL different concentrations of cholesterol (in 1% Triton X100) and 350 μL Phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) were mixed and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, DACNF/FeNCDs strip, 100 μL of 10 mM ABTS and 1400 μL sodium acetate buffer (pH = 3.6) were added to the above mixture. The mixture was incubated at 30 °C for 20 min. Then, the absorbance of the treated final mixtures was recorded.

Human serum samples were obtained from Chungnam National University Hospital Biobank, a member of the Korea Biobank Network. 50 μL of 70 UN/mL cholesterol oxidase, 50 μL of 50 UN/mL cholesterol esterase and 100 μL of human serum sample were added to 300 μL Phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Rest of the procedure was similar to cholesterol detection as mention above. The study was approved by the ethics committees of the institutions involved.

FeNCDs were prepared according to a previous report on NCDs preparation with slight modifications. The hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA), ethylene diamine and FeCl2•4H2O at 160 °C for 4 h resulted FeNCDs. Dialysis is used for purification instead of precipitation in alcohol. This is because; unreacted Fe2+ ions may co-precipitate and contaminate the product and dialysis is the most widely accepted way of purifying CDs. With the aim of tuning the Fe content and peroxidase activity of FeNCDs, the precursor mole ratios (CA to FeCl2•4H2O) were varied in the range of 10:1 to 10:5. Surprisingly, with the increase in the Fe precursor content, no significant difference in the peroxidase activity as well as emission properties were observed (Figure S1). But the product yield varied significantly. With increase in the precursor mole ratio from 10:1 to 10:3, the yield increased from 1.1% to 6.16%. Further increase in the mole ratio resulted in the char formation and decreased the yield. Interestingly no significant formation of NCDs was observed in the absence of FeCl2•4H2O. However good yield was observed in the previous study where the purification was done by precipitation [23]. Hence it is assumed that, without Fe doping very small nanoparticles that could pass through the pores of dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500 Da) were formed. Iron doping might have increased the particle size so that they will remain in the dialysis bag. With increase in the Fe precursor content more number of bigger particles might be formed and that improved the yield. When Fe content is above a limit, the growing particles (due their higher number) might combine with one another to form bigger aggregates that ultimately results in char formation and decreased the FeNCDs yield.

Periodate oxidation of CNF was performed to produce dialdehyde functions. Washing by centrifugation was resulted in great aggregation of fibrils. Hence, washing was done by mixing with large quantity of water, allowing settling down and removing the supernatant. This process was repeated three times. Yield was above 90%, and aldehyde content calculated from titration method was found to be 1.25 ± 0.12 mM/g. In view of the tendency of dialdehyde functions to form hemiacetals with adjacent OH groups and become unavailable for reaction, [25] all the experiments were performed with fresh DACNF (not more than 10 days old).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of FeNCDs ( Fig. 1
a) displayed well dispersed spherical particles without any aggregation. Size distribution histogram ( Fig. 1
b) revealed that the particles were uniformly distributed in the size range of 2 – 10 nm with an average diameter of 6.0 ± 1.7 nm. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) was not fruitful which might be due to their amorphous structure. TEM image of PCNF ( Fig. 1
c) showed well dispersed fibrils with diameters in the range of 6 – 10 nm. After periodate oxidation, only slight changes were observed indicating the fiber structure retained ( Fig. 1
d). In contrast, aggregation and fiber shortening was observed in several previous reports. Study by Hohyun Lee et al. [24], revealed that with increase in the extent of periodate oxidation, CNF will become short, aggregated and finally look like CNC. In view of this, low periodate content and short reaction time were chosen so that the fiber morphology can be retained. Because of the small size, low contrast and disk like structure, FeNCDs were not appeared in the TEM images of DACNF/FeNCDs.

In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of FeNCDs ( Fig. 1
e), peak around 2 theta 9° can be assigned to graphene oxide structure and halo at around 20 – 30° indicating its amorphous nature [26]. Raman spectrum (Figure S2) exhibited both D (disordered) and G (graphitic) bands of graphene and ID/IG ratio (ratio of integrated area of D and G peaks) is greater than 1 (1.24) indicating the amorphous nature [27]. These results confirm the structural similarities of FeNCDs with graphene. XRD patterns of PCNF ( Fig. 1
f) displayed the characteristic cellulose I peaks at 2θ around 15.3 and 22.5° corresponding to (110) and (200) reflections [28]. Both DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs were also presented similar peaks indicating that the periodate oxidation and subsequent FeNCDs binding have no effect on the cellulose crystal structure. However, after periodate oxidation, the relative crystallinity calculated by Segal equation (CrI%) was significantly decreased from 68% to 57%. This decrease might be due to the opening of glucopyranose ring and destruction of aligned packaging [24] and this behavior is in well accordance with the literature [29,30]. Interestingly, after FeNCDs attachment, a small increase in the relative crystallinity from 57% to 62% was observed. This might be due to the crosslinking effect of FeNCDs. It is expected that the nanoparticle surface will have more than one amine function with which it can bind to the aldehyde groups of two adjacent fibers and results in crosslinked structures. This crosslinking makes the fibers more aligned and might result in improved CrI.

In the FTIR spectrum of FeNCDs ( Fig. 1
g), broad peak in the range of 3000 – 3600 cm−1 correspond to –OH and –NH groups. Intense peaks at 1550 and 1360 cm−1 can be assigned to C – N and C = O stretch, whereas the peaks at 1250 and 1064 cm−1 can be ascribed to C – O and C – C stretch [31]. These observations conclude that the FeNCDs possess abundant surface functional groups with which they can bind to CNF. In the FTIR spectrum of PCNF ( Fig. 1
h), the broad absorption band around 3330 cm−1 corresponds to OH and the peak at 2900 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching. Small peak at 1645 cm−1 correspond to the adsorbed moisture [32]. The peak at 1315 cm−1 correlates with CH2 wagging, and the peaks at 1160 and 1026 cm−1 were assigned to the C–O asymmetric stretch and valence vibrations, respectively. The peak at 895 cm−1 was assigned to the C–H deformation vibrations of cellulose. After periodate oxidation, a new peak appeared at 1745 cm−1 clearly indicated the formation of aldehyde function [33]. Further, the intensity of peak at 1160 cm−1 corresponding to COH stretch got decreased indicating the consumption of -OH groups in the oxidation process. In the DACNF/FeNCDs sample, the peak corresponding to the aldehyde groups got disappeared. This observation confirms the consumption of aldehyde functions in the binding with FeNCDs. Due to the low loading content, peaks corresponding to FeNCDs were not observed in the FTIR spectrum of DACNF/FeNCDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis of FeNCDs was performed to probe the chemical composition and surface chemical states. As shown in Fig. 2
a, XPS survey scan of FeNCDs revealed the presence of peaks corresponding to C 1 s (285 eV), O 1 s (530 eV), N 1 s (400) and Fe 2p (740 eV). The chemical composition was found to be C 50.63%, O 35.13%, N 8.87% and Fe 5.37%. Presence of Fe and N peaks confirms the successful doping of iron and N in FeNCDs. The Fe content is high compared to previous reports [34,35] and it is beneficial for achieving high catalytic activity. Deconvolution of high resolution Fe 2p spectrum resulted in four peaks ( Fig. 2
b) corresponding to Fe2+ 2p3/2 (710 eV), Fe3+ 2p3/2 (714 eV), Fe2+ 2p1/2 (723 eV) and Fe3+ 2p1/2 (727 eV) [31]. The appearance of Fe3+ peaks (even the Fe2+ precursor is used) indicated the partial oxidation of Fe2+ during the hydrothermal reaction. Presence of iron in two oxidation states is highly beneficial for peroxidase activity and Fenton like reaction. As shown in Fig. 2
c, high resolution C 1 s spectrum can be resolved into three peaks corresponding to CC/C = C at 284.7 eV, CO/CN at 286.1 eV and C = O at 288.2 eV. N 1 s spectrum can be resolved into two peaks corresponding to the presence of CN-C at 399.6 eV and NH at 401.3 eV. These results confirm the presence of amine functional groups on FeNCDs with which they can bind to the aldehyde functions of DACNF. Comparison of the XPS survey spectra of DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs is shown in Fig. 2
d. The peaks corresponding to C and O are observed in both the samples. In addition, the DACNF/FeNCDs exhibited the N1s peak which can correlate to the presence of FeNCDs. Peaks related to Fe cannot be seen due to the low loading content. From the elemental composition, the O/C ratio of DACNF was found to be 0.64 and it was decreased to 0.60 for DACNF/FeNCDs. Comparison of the high resolution N1s spectrum of FeNCDs with that of DACNF/FeNCDs clearly revealed the binding interaction. As shown in Fig. 2
e, compared to FeNCDs, a drastic decrease in the amine peak intensity is observed in DACNF/FeNCDs which confirm the consumption of amine groups in the binding with DACNF.

CDs are well known for their fluorescence properties. As shown Fig. 3
a, the aqueous dispersion of FeNCDs exhibited light brown color under day light and displayed bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Absorption spectrum displayed no characteristic peak but exhibited absorption in the UV region. Excitation spectrum exhibited an intense peak at 380 nm and corresponding emission peak is appeared at 465 nm with a stoke shift of 85 nm. Fluoresce quantum yield (FL QY) measured using quinine sulfate as a reference was found to be 15.5% (Figure S3). This is highly advantageous for other biological applications where both catalytic activity and FL emission are crucial. CDs are well known for their characteristic excitation dependent emission [36]. Hence the FL emission is recorded at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 460 nm. As shown in Fig. 3
b, along with emission intensity, change in the emission peak position is also observed. Normalized emission spectra shown in Figure S4 clearly displayed this red shift. With change in the excitation from 300 to 460 nm, emission peak position shifted from 440 to 522 nm. Digital photographs of DACNF and DACNF/FeNCDs films are shown in Fig. 3
c. Under ambient light, DACNF film is white transparent whereas DACNF/FeNCDs film is light brown and transparent. UV light images clearly presented bright emission exclusively from DACNF/FeNCDs indicating the presence of FeNCDs. Bright and uniform fluorescence without any spots indicates that the FeNCDs are uniformly distributed throughout the film and the optical properties are not deteriorated by the chemical linkage to cellulose. This behavior is quite useful in developing fluorescent packages and anti-counterfeiting applications.

Peroxidase activity of FeNCDs was tested by the H2O2 mediated oxidation of chromogenic substrate ABTS to blue colored ox-ABTS. As shown in the Fig. 4
a, 0.25 mM ABTS solution in acetate buffer (pH 3.6), in combination with H2O2 (0.1 mM) remained colourless even after 10 min. Whereas with the addition of 10 µg FeNCDs, the same solution produced bright blue color in the same time and exhibited a strong absorption peak at 417 nm. This observation indicates the peroxidase activity of FeNCDs. Peroxidase activity of FeNCDs was further confirmed by testing with two other chromogenic substances i.e. OPD and TMB. As shown in Fig. 4
b and
4
c, both the chromogenic substances produced respective colours and characteristic absorption peaks. This demonstrates the broad-spectrum peroxidase activity of FeNCDs which is not specific to a particular substrate [22]. High response was observed for ABTS and hence, further studies were performed with ABTS. In most of the previous reports high response was observed for TMB over ABTS due to its high εmax value (TMB 39,000 M−1cm−1; ABTS 36,000 M−1cm−1). However, in our case ABTS was found to be more responsive which might be due to the solvent used. As ABTS is water soluble, aqueous solution is used, whereas TMB is used in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). As FeNCDs are highly hydrophilic in nature, the reaction with ABTS might be faster over TMB.

Steady state kinetic analysis was carried out to verify the catalytic performance of FeNCDs. Typical Michaelis-Menten curves ( Fig. 4
d and
4
g) for H2O2 and ABTS were generated by plotting the initial reaction rates against various concentrations of one substrate while keeping other substrate constant. Thus obtained curves were fitted to the double reciprocal Line weaver-Burk plots and the common kinetic parameters i.e. Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) were calculated ( Fig. 4
e and
5
g). These values are listed in Table 1
and compared with various other nanozymes and Horseradish peroxidase (HRP). It is widely known that Km is a constant that reflects the binding affinity between enzyme and substrate and low Km value indicates high affinity [37]. Whereas the Vmax value reveals the turnover number of the enzyme and high Vmax values are desired [38]. The Km value of FeNCDs for H2O2 was found six times lower than that of HRP suggesting the superior capacity of FeNCDs for activating H2O2. The Vmax of FeNCDs for H2O2 was found 4.8 times higher than that of HRP. This low Km and high Vmax values suggest the higher catalytic ability of FeNCDs over HRP and it can be ascribed to their high Fe content and small size that offer high active catalytic surface. The fitted double reciprocal plots ( Fig. 4
f and
4
i) at different ABTS and H2O2 concentrations were appeared nearly parallel indicating the involvement of typical Ping-Pong mechanism. This means the FeNCDs will bind to and react with the first substrate, and release the product before combining with the second substrate similar to HRP [39]. The nanozyme activity (bnanozyme ) and specific activity (ananozyme ) were calculated according to the protocol suggested by Bing Jiang et al. [40]. From the kinetic plots shown in Fig. 5
a, the initial reaction rates (ΔA/Δt) with 4, 8, 12, and 16 µg of FeNCDs were calculated first. By substituting these values in the given equation (given in SI), bnanozyme is then calculated. As shown in Fig. 5
b, the plot of bnanozyme vs FeNCDs mass is found linear and ananozyme calculated from the slope of the linear fit is 3.654 U/mg.

Peroxidase activity of CDs prepared by varying the precursors (Table S1) was tested to gain some insights in to the mechanism (complete details are provided in SI). Under the same experimental conditions, NCDs (without Fe) failed to exhibit significant peroxidase activity indicating that Fe active site is crucial in peroxidase activity. The mechanism of peroxidase activity of Fe containing materials generally involves the Fenton's reaction, where H2O2 will be broken down to produce OH radicals which will further lead to the oxidation of chromogenic substance [50]. The production of OH radicals can be tested by using the well-known terepthalic acid (TA) experiment. TA will interact with hydroxyl radicals and produce hydroxy TA (HTA) which has emission at 425 nm under the excitation of 315 nm. This fluorescence signal enables the detection of hydroxyl radical production [15]. The mixture of FeNCDs and H2O2 was added to TA, incubated for 12 h and the emission spectra were recorded ( Fig. 5
c). Appearance of emission peak centered at 425 nm (at λex 315 nm) whose intensity increased with increase in the amount of FeNCDs confirmed the production of hydroxyl radicals. To avoid the interference of FeNCDs emission, emission spectra of control samples (having same amount FeNCDs and without TA) were also recorded and subtracted. H2O2 assisted dye degradation experiments were conducted to further probe the role of OH radicals. As shown in Fig. 5
d and Figure S5 both Rhodamine B (RhB) and methylene blue (MB) were successfully degraded only in the co-presence of FeNCDs and H2O2, indicating hydroxyl radical generation. Interestingly, complete degradation of both dyes was observed within 2 h of reaction signifying the great potential of FeNCDs in the Fenton like catalytic dye degradation. Hydroxyl radical formation was further confirmed by Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy using DMPO as trapping agent. DMPO reacts with OH radicals and forms DMPO/OH spin adduct which exhibit a typical four lines ESR spectrum with relative intensities of 1:2:2:1 [51]. As shown in Fig. 5
e, the characteristic four lines spectrum is observed only for H2O2+FeNCDs+DMPO system but not for H2O2+DMPO that confirmed the generation of OH radicals by FeNCDs.

As the CDs are fluorescent nanomaterials and have semiconducting nature, it is quite common to think about the influence of light on its peroxidase activity. To shed light on this, the peroxidase activity was tested under different lighting conditions including dark, ambient light, 365 nm UV light and 254 nm UV light. As H2O2 tend to decompose under UV light, control experiments were conducted in the absence of catalyst and the absorbance values were subtracted. Interestingly, almost a two fold increase in the activity was observed under UV light (both under 365 and 254 nm) irradiation ( Fig. 5
f). This observation indicates that the photoexcitation facilitates the efficient electron transfer process and enhances the catalytic oxidation. This feature is advantageous as the natural enzyme HRP will undergo deactivation (photodenaturation) under UV irradiation. Furthermore, this will open up the future applicability of FeNCDs in Photo-Fenton process and light activated antimicrobials.

Likewise, peroxidase activity of the DACNF/FeNCDs nanohybrid film was studied by ABTS oxidation. The film (diameter 35 mm; thickness 40 µ) was cut into small circular strips (diameter, 6 mm each) with an office punch and utilized as the recyclable nanozyme. Firstly, the peroxidase activity was confirmed by adding DACNF/FeNCDs strips to the buffer solutions containing H2O2, ABTS and ABTS+H2O2. Figure S6a shows that only the DACNF/FeNCDs strip in the (ABTS +H2O2) system was produced blue color, indicating its peroxidase activity and successful immobilization of FeNCDs. Variation of peroxidase activity as a function of FeNCDs content was further studied. As shown in Figure S6b, peroxidase activity was increased with increase in FeNCDs loading up to 2% (wt% with respect to DACNF dry weight). However, no big difference was observed beyond 2% and at high loading content the film became brittle. Hence 2% was selected as optimum.

Peroxidase activity of FeNCDs liquid sample and DACNF/FeNCDs were compared to evaluate the effect of FeCNDs immobilization on peroxidase activity. As shown in Figure S7a, the activity of FeNCDs retained only 48% after immobilization on DACNF. This significant decrease might be due to the fact that the rate of heterogeneous catalysis will be much lower than that of homogenous catalysis. Though immobilization results in decreased catalytic activity, it offers recyclability. Under the same conditions, PCNF/FeNCDs strips were also prepared and tested for peroxidase activity. Compared to DACNF/FeNCDs, only 21.3% of activity was exhibited by PCNF/FeNCDs (Figure S7b). This indicates the significance of CNF chemical modification in FeNCDs immobilization.

Peroxidase activity of DACNF/FeNCDs nanohybrid was optimized by varying different parameters. As shown in Figure S8a, 20 min of incubation time was found optimal for producing maximum activity. Just like natural enzyme, the catalytic activity of the DACNF/FeNCDs nanozyme was also found to be influenced by reaction conditions such as pH and temperature. As protons also participates in peroxidase reaction, the acidic pH was found suitable and the activity decreased with increase in the pH from 2.6 to 5.6 (Figure S8b). However, to avoid harsh acidic conditions, pH 3.6 is selected as optimum. The nanozyme exhibited good activity in the temperature range of 20 – 40 °C (Figure S8c), and highest activity was observed at 30 °C. Furthermore, 0.5 mM ABTS was found optimal for the assay (Figure S8d). As a whole, pH 3.6, room temperature, 0.5 mM ABTS and 20 min of incubation time were selected as optimal conditions for further studies.

H2O2 functions as an important signaling molecule in cells and involved in a variety of biological activities [52]. Hence the quantification of H2O2 in biological samples is of great significance. The DACNF/FeNCDs catalysed oxidation of ABTS and subsequent absorbance rise was found to be H2O2 concentration dependent. On this basis, a colorimetric H2O2 detection assay is developed. As shown in Fig. 6
a, under optimal conditions, intensity of the characteristic absorption peak increased gradually with increase in H2O2 concentration. Meanwhile, the solution color turned from colourless towards deep blue. The plot of absorbance at 417 nm (A417nm) vs H2O2 concentration ( Fig. 6
b) presented a linear response in the H2O2 concentration range of 10 – 80 µM. The linear regression equation to fit the data is A417nm = 0.00848 [H2O2] + 0.10645. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated from the equation 3 s/k (where s standard deviation of blank signal and k is the slope of the linear fit) was found to be 0.70 µM.

Inspired by the H2O2 detection performance, a colorimetric cholesterol detection assay was developed by introducing the cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) enzyme into the DACNF/FeNCDs and ABTS system. In a typical assay, in the presence of oxygen, ChOx converts cholesterol to cholest-en-3-one and produces H2O2 [53]. Subsequently, the DACNF/FeNCDs nanozyme produces hydroxyl radicals by decomposing the in-situ generated H2O2 which in turn oxidizes the colorless ABTS to blue colored ox-ABTS. This way, ABTS absorbance will be directly proportional to the cholesterol concentration. As shown in Fig. 6
c, the characteristic peak intensity is increased with increase in Cholesterol concentration and linear response was observed in the range of 50 to 400 µM ( Fig. 6
d). The linear regression to fit the data is A417nm = 0.00161[Ch] + 0.10262. The LOD calculated from the equation 3 s/k (where s is the standard deviation of blank signal and k is the slope of the linear fit) was found to be 3.72 µM. Analytical performance of the current probe is compared with previous reports. As shown in the Table 2
, DACNF/FeNCDs nanozyme exhibited good LOD and decent linear range.

Selectivity to the target analyte is an indispensable feature of a probe. The selectivity of the current method is evaluated by studying the interference of small molecules and ions that are commonly present in biological samples. As shown in Fig. 6
e no significant interference was observed even if the interfereant concentration was 10 times higher than that of cholesterol. These results highlighted the high selectivity and practical applicability of the current method. It is worth mentioning that the FL emission of FeNCDs and DACNF/FeNCDs was not disturbed by cholesterol (Figure S9). Practical applicability of the present method is evaluated by analysing human serum samples. In serum, cholesterol exists in the form of free cholesterol and cholesterol ester. By introducing cholesterol esterase, free cholesterol can be released from cholesterol ester [62]. Therefore, total cholesterol in serum samples can be detected with the help of cholesterol esterase and cholesterol oxidase. The results of the human serum sample analysis are given in Table 3
. It is worth mentioning that the results are in well accordance with the results provided by hospital. Furthermore the probe was able to detect cholesterol in butter samples with satisfactory results (Table S2).

Stability of DACNF/FeNCDs was evaluated by storing it at ambient conditions and measuring the peroxidase activity at regular intervals. As shown in Fig. 6
f, the activity still remained over 90% even after the storage for 50 days indicating the great storage stability. Stability in buffer solution was also evaluated by incubating in acetate buffer (pH 3.6) for different time intervals. As shown in Figure S10, the catalyst was able to retain 87% of activity even after 5 h of incubation. This high stability is an advantageous feature and can be attributed to the uniform dispersion in nanocellulose matrix which prevents FeNCDs from aggregation. Most of the nanozyme based literature is focused on the usage colloidal nanozymes and their possible reusability is rarely explored. Though some efforts were made using nanowires[63], and magnetic nanomaterials [64] they require techniques like ultracentrifugation or magnetic separation which require high energy and costly instruments. In view of this, developing a reusable nanozyme with easy recyclability is highly desired [65]. DACNF/FeNCDs nanozyme strips are easy to handle and do not require any instrumental or time consuming techniques. They can be simply removed with tweezers and washed and reused. As shown in Fig. 6
g the spent catalyst retained more than 90% activity even after 10 cycles of use. Moreover the catalyst after 10 cycles of usage is characterized using FTIR and ICP-OES analysis. FTIR (Figure S11) revealed that there are no significant changes in functional groups implying the no changes in chemical functions of the nanohybrid. Fe content in DACNF/FeNCDs film, before and catalysis was determined using ICP-OES to probe the possible leaching of FeNCDs. Interestingly, only an 8% decrease in the Fe content is observed indicating the high stability. With these advantages of mild preparation conditions, good product yields, high activity, recyclability and ability to detect cholesterol in real samples, the DACNF/FeNCDs nanozyme is considered economic and provide great promise for future commercialization.

A one-step hydrothermal method is demonstrated for the synthesis of Fe, N-doped carbon dots (FeNCDs) with high peroxidase activity and good fluorescence quantum yield. Fe precursor content variation has not influenced the fluorescence and peroxidase activity but improved the product yield. Excellent peroxidase activity is evident from low Km and high Vmax values for both substrates, and good specific activity of 3654 U/g. Terepthalic acid, dye degradation and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopic studies confirmed the involvement of hydroxyl radicals in peroxidase activity. Periodate oxidation of cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) was confirmed by the appearance of carbonyl peak and decreased crystallinity index. FeNCDs immobilized on dialdehyde CNF (DACNF) via Schiff base formation and reductive amination were uniformly dispersed throughout the DACNF matrix, and caused no disturbance to the cellulose structure. Immobilization of FeNCDs on DACNF prevented them from aggregation and retained their fluorescence and peroxidase activity. Based on the peroxidase activity, the DACNF/FeNCDs composite film was applied for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and cholesterol using ABTS as chromogen. DACNF functioned as an impressive support and rendered the nanozyme with easy recovery, reusability and long shelf life. Further the probe presented satisfactory results for cholesterol quantification in human serum samples. This work will pave the way for developing various strategies for immobilization of CDs based nanozymes and exploration of their wide applications.
